name: Lead Capture
objective: Get every enquiry into one place, attributed, without anyone retyping it.
commonProblems: Enquiries arrive across a shared mailbox, phone, WhatsApp and personal inboxes with no single record
Source attribution is guessed after the fact, so marketing spend cannot be judged
Web form data is emailed rather than written to the CRM
Duplicates are created because nobody checks first
Leads sit unassigned over a weekend and go cold
futureState: Lead record created automatically with source captured
Duplicate check against existing contacts and accounts
Assignment by rule (territory, product, round-robin or account manager)
Owner notified with a response deadline
controls: Duplicate detection on email, phone and company
Assignment rule with a fallback owner
risks: Personal inboxes stay outside the system and the record is incomplete
Channel integrations (WhatsApp, social) depend on third-party approval the client has not started
Source is captured but never used, so the field decays
discoveryQuestions: Every channel an enquiry can arrive on — including the ones that are not official?
Who owns the shared mailbox today, and what do they do with a message?
How do you know which marketing activity produced this enquiry?
What counts as a duplicate here — same person, same company, or same opportunity?
What is the expected first response time, and who is accountable for it?

name: Lead Qualification
objective: Decide early, and on stated criteria, which enquiries deserve the team's time.
commonProblems: Every enquiry is treated as a live opportunity, so the pipeline is fiction
Qualification criteria live in the manager's head
Nothing is ever marked lost, so the pipeline only grows
Unqualified leads are converted to deals to look busy
The reason for losing a lead is not recorded, so the pattern is never seen
futureState: Lead received and reviewed against defined criteria
Qualified / not qualified decision with a reason
Qualified lead converts to a deal with mandatory data
Not qualified is closed with a reason code
Nurture path for 'not now' rather than 'not ever'
dataRequired: Budget / need / authority / timing indicators if used
risks: Qualification becomes a formality that everyone clicks through
'Not now' leads disappear instead of entering nurture
Criteria are too rigid for an exception-heavy business
discoveryQuestions: What makes an enquiry worth pursuing here — specifically?
Who decides, and can that decision be overruled?
What happens to a lead that is real but not ready?
How many contact attempts before you stop?
What did you lose last month, and why?

name: Lead-to-Deal
objective: Convert a qualified lead into a deal without losing the history that made it qualified.
commonProblems: Conversion loses the enquiry history and the source
Deals are created directly, bypassing the lead stage entirely
Account, contact and deal are duplicated at conversion
The deal has no value or close date, so forecasting is impossible
Different associates convert at different points, so stage data is not comparable
futureState: Duplicate check against existing accounts and contacts
Convert to account, contact and deal in one action
Mandatory deal data captured (value, expected close, product interest)
Deal enters the pipeline at a defined stage
risks: Existing account data is dirty, so duplicate checking produces noise
Sales resists mandatory fields and enters placeholder values
Conversion timing differs by person and skews every downstream metric
discoveryQuestions: At what exact moment does an enquiry become a deal here?
What must be known before it can be one?
Is the customer already in another system under a different name?
Who is allowed to create a deal without a lead?
What does the pipeline report need that conversion must capture?

name: Opportunity Management
objective: Keep the pipeline honest: a stage should mean the same thing to everyone.
commonProblems: Stages describe internal activity rather than customer commitment
Deals sit in a stage for months with no next action
Forecast is a manager's instinct, not a system output
Stage movement has no exit criteria, so it means nothing
Won deals are not handed over to delivery in any structured way
futureState: Stage progression against defined exit criteria
Mandatory next action on every open deal
Won or lost with a reason
Structured handover to delivery on won
controls: Approval on discount or non-standard terms
risks: Stage definitions get diluted until the pipeline is decorative
Forecast pressure encourages optimistic close dates
The handover to delivery is assumed rather than designed
discoveryQuestions: What has to be true for a deal to move to the next stage?
Who is allowed to change the close date?
How is a quotation produced today, and in which system?
What happens the moment a deal is won?
What does the sales manager look at every Monday, and where does it come from?

name: Quotation Management
objective: Produce an accurate quote fast, and know which system is allowed to price it.
commonProblems: Quoting takes half an hour because pricing, stock and margin live in three places
Two systems can both produce a quote, and they disagree
Revisions are manual and version history is lost
Discounts are applied without approval
The CRM has no record that a quote was ever sent
futureState: Pricing and stock checked in the system that owns them
Quote generated in the pricing system of record
Quote reference and value written back to the CRM
Approved or rejected state recorded against the deal
controls: Mandatory quote reference on the deal
risks: Two systems both claim the right to price, and reconciliation becomes a permanent manual task
Real-time stock and price sync is assumed but the API rate limits do not allow it
Sales continues to quote in a spreadsheet because it is faster
discoveryQuestions: Which system produces the official quotation, and who says so?
Where do price, cost, stock and margin actually come from?
How long does a quote take today, and where does the time go?
Who can approve a discount, and at what threshold?
What happens to the quote number when the quote is revised?
Does the salesperson need live stock, or stock as at this morning?

name: Customer Onboarding
objective: Turn a won deal into a working customer relationship without a gap in the middle.
commonProblems: Won deals are handed over by email, or not at all
The customer repeats information they already gave sales
Nobody owns the customer between signature and first delivery
Onboarding steps live in a checklist on someone's desktop
Billing setup and operational setup happen in the wrong order
futureState: Data and document collection from the customer
Handover to support and account management
risks: Sales considers the job finished at signature
Customer document collection stalls and no one escalates it
Onboarding is designed for the ideal customer and breaks on the second one
discoveryQuestions: What has to happen between signature and the customer's first real interaction?
Who owns the customer during that window?
What do you ask the customer for, and how often do they send it late?
What did sales promise that operations has to deliver?
When is onboarding 'done'?

name: Renewal Management
objective: Stop renewals from depending on someone remembering.
commonProblems: Renewal dates live in a spreadsheet nobody opens
The first reminder is the expiry
Renewal is a re-sale from scratch because usage history is not visible
Auto-renewal terms are not tracked, so notice periods are missed
Lapsed customers are never followed up
futureState: Automated advance notification to owner and customer
Renewed, renegotiated or lapsed with a reason
risks: Renewal data has to be migrated from spreadsheets and is unreliable at the start
Notice periods differ per contract and were never captured
Automated customer reminders go out before the account manager has looked
discoveryQuestions: Where do renewal dates live today?
How far in advance should the business act?
Do any agreements auto-renew, and what notice applies?
Who is allowed to change renewal terms?
What do you do about a customer who lapsed last year?

name: Customer Nurturing
objective: Stay useful to the customers who are not ready to buy today.
commonProblems: 'Not now' leads are closed and forgotten
Nurture is a single newsletter to everybody
Sales and marketing contact the same person about different things on the same day
There is no exit from nurture back into an active sales conversation
Unsubscribes are handled inconsistently across channels
futureState: Contact enters nurture with a reason and a segment
Segment-appropriate content on a defined cadence
Engagement tracked against the CRM record
Contact returns to active pipeline or continues
Suppression and consent respected across all channels
controls: Single view of customer contact across sales and marketing
risks: Content production is assumed to exist and does not
Consent records are incomplete after migration
Marketing and sales automation both message the same contact
discoveryQuestions: Who writes the content, and does that person exist yet?
What are the real segments — not the ones on the slide?
What should make a nurtured contact become a live lead again?
How is consent recorded today, per channel?
Who is allowed to email this list?

name: Enquiry-to-Resolution
objective: One journey from 'a customer said something' to 'it is closed and they know'.
commonProblems: Enquiries arrive on WhatsApp, social, email and phone and only some become tickets
The same customer is handled by three people who cannot see each other's replies
Resolution is a conversation, so there is no record of what was agreed
There is no definition of 'resolved', so tickets close early
The customer chases because nobody told them anything
futureState: Enquiry captured from any channel as a ticket
Action taken by the responsible team
Closure with a reason and category
risks: Channel integrations depend on third-party approval (business messaging, social) outside the project's control
Agents keep answering on the channel directly because it is faster
Categories proliferate until reporting is meaningless
discoveryQuestions: Every way a customer can reach you — including the ones you wish they did not use?
What happens today when the same customer messages twice on different channels?
Who decides priority?
What does 'resolved' mean here, and who says so?
What are the top five things customers contact you about?
What does the customer see and hear during the wait?

name: Ticket Management
objective: Give every request an owner, a state and a deadline.
commonProblems: Tickets are assigned to a shared queue that everyone assumes someone else is watching
Status values describe the agent's mood rather than the state of the work
Waiting on the customer still counts against response time
Reassignment loses context
Reopened tickets are counted as new
futureState: Auto-assignment by category, product or language
Internal notes separated from customer replies
Waiting states that pause the SLA clock
dataRequired: Customer and related order or asset
reporting: Open tickets by status, team and age
risks: A status model designed by managers does not survive contact with agents
Auto-assignment sends work to people on leave
Every exception becomes a new status
discoveryQuestions: What states can a ticket be in — and which of those are the same thing under different names?
How is a ticket assigned today?
What happens when an agent is on leave or leaves the company?
When are you waiting on the customer, and should that count against you?
What has to be true before a ticket can close?

name: SLA & Escalation
objective: Make the promise explicit, then make the system enforce it.
commonProblems: SLAs exist in a contract and nowhere in the system
Escalation means shouting across the office
The clock runs at 2am and over public holidays
Breach is discovered in the monthly report
Everything is priority one
futureState: SLA target applied per priority and customer tier
Business hours and holiday calendar respected
Warning before breach to the owner
Escalation to team lead on threshold
reporting: SLA compliance by priority and team
risks: Business hours differ by country and nobody has confirmed the calendar
Escalation notifications become noise and are filtered to a folder
Priority is set by whoever shouts loudest, not by the matrix
discoveryQuestions: What have you actually promised customers, in writing?
Do different customers get different promises?
What are your support hours, in each country you operate in?
When the clock is about to run out, who should know first?
What has happened historically when you breached?

name: Complaint Management
objective: Handle the ones that matter differently from the ones that are routine.
commonProblems: A complaint is logged as a normal ticket and disappears into the queue
There is no route to management for a serious complaint
Root cause is never captured, so the same complaint recurs
Compensation or goodwill is granted inconsistently
Regulatory-relevant complaints are not distinguishable
futureState: Complaint identified and flagged as such
Resolution and remedy agreed within authority limits
Closed with root cause and preventive action
controls: Complaint flag distinct from ticket type
reporting: Complaints by category and root cause
risks: Root cause is recorded as 'customer error' by default
Remedy authority is undefined, so agents either over-give or escalate everything
Complaint volume is politically uncomfortable and gets reclassified
discoveryQuestions: What separates a complaint from a normal support request here?
Which complaints must a manager see, and how fast?
Who can offer a refund, a credit or goodwill, and up to what value?
Is any of this regulated, and does anything have to be reported?
What is the complaint you receive most often, and why does it keep happening?

name: Return & Exchange
objective: Decide the outcome once, then let stock and finance follow automatically.
commonProblems: Return, exchange, refund and replacement are all called 'a return'
Stock is not adjusted until someone remembers
The customer is refunded before the goods arrive back — or long after
Eligibility is decided per agent
Marketplace and direct returns follow different rules with no reason
futureState: Return authorised with an RMA reference
Outcome decided: refund, exchange, repair or reject
controls: Written eligibility policy encoded as rules
reporting: Return rate by product and reason
risks: Reverse logistics is assumed to work like forward logistics and does not
Refund timing conflicts between customer expectation and finance policy
Marketplace rules override internal policy and were not in scope
discoveryQuestions: What outcomes are actually possible — refund, exchange, repair, replace, reject?
Who decides, and against what policy?
When does stock come back into inventory, and in what condition state?
When is the customer refunded — on request, on collection or on inspection?
Do marketplace or channel rules override yours?
Who pays for the return leg?

name: Defect & Damage Resolution
objective: Separate 'the product is faulty' from 'it broke in transit' — because the money and the fix are different.
commonProblems: Damage in transit and manufacturing defect are handled identically
Evidence is requested inconsistently, so claims fail later
Courier claim windows expire before anyone files
Supplier recovery is never pursued
The inspection outcome is an opinion in an email
futureState: Classified as defect, transit damage or misuse
Recovery claim raised against the liable party
controls: Mandatory evidence per class (photographs, packaging, batch)
reporting: Defect rate by product, batch and supplier
Transit damage by courier and route
risks: Inspection depends on people outside the system (couriers, suppliers, third-party inspectors)
Claim windows are short and the process is slow
Cascading classification rules become unmaintainable if not designed as data
discoveryQuestions: What are the genuinely different failure types, and does the money differ per type?
What evidence do you need, and who is going to ask the customer for it?
Who inspects, and are they internal?
How long do you have to claim against a courier or supplier?
Who decides liability when the evidence is ambiguous?

name: After-Sales Servicing
objective: Own the relationship after the invoice, especially when someone else owns the payment.
commonProblems: The customer does not know who to call after purchase — the seller, the merchant or the finance provider
Warranty terms are not visible to the person answering the phone
Service history is not attached to the asset
Third-party merchants set their own service standards
Servicing and collections conversations get confused
futureState: Responsibility determined between seller, merchant and provider
Work completed and recorded against the asset
Cost allocated to the liable party
controls: Service history against the asset, not the ticket
reporting: Service volume by product and merchant
Repeat service on the same asset
risks: Responsibility between marketplace, merchant and finance provider is genuinely ambiguous and must be decided commercially, not technically
Warranty data does not exist for historical sales
Field capacity is not modelled
discoveryQuestions: After the sale, who does the customer believe they are dealing with?
Who is contractually responsible for servicing on each channel?
Where is warranty information today?
Does the asset have an identity in your system, or only the order?
Who pays when the merchant will not?

name: Form-to-CRM
objective: Make a web form create a real record, not an email.
commonProblems: Form submissions arrive as email and are retyped
The form asks for data nobody uses and omits data everybody needs
There is no link between the submission and the campaign that produced it
Submissions from a logged-in user do not link to their existing record
Spam floods the pipeline
futureState: Record created or matched in the CRM
Hidden context fields captured (campaign, source, referring record ID)
dataRequired: Hidden record IDs where the form is pre-linked
risks: Hidden CRM IDs passed through a form can be tampered with and must be validated server-side
The form is owned by a web agency outside the project
Consent wording is a legal question, not a design one
discoveryQuestions: Where does this form live, and who controls the page?
What must exist in the CRM before this form can be linked to it?
Which fields are actually used downstream — and which are habit?
What happens if the same person submits twice?
What consent are you capturing, and who approved the wording?

name: Campaign Segmentation
objective: Send the right thing to a defined group, and be able to explain who was in it.
commonProblems: Segments are built by exporting to a spreadsheet and filtering by hand
The list is rebuilt from scratch every campaign
Suppression and consent are applied inconsistently
Segment criteria are not recorded, so results cannot be compared
The CRM and the campaign tool hold different versions of the same person
futureState: Segment defined by criteria against CRM data
Segment synced to the campaign tool
Engagement written back to the CRM record
Segment criteria retained for repeat and comparison
controls: Consent check as a hard rule
One direction of sync per field
risks: Data quality determines segment quality and is usually worse than assumed
Two-way sync between CRM and campaign tools creates conflicts unless one side owns each field
Consent history does not survive migration
discoveryQuestions: How do you build a list today, step by step?
Which system owns the contact, and which one owns the consent?
How often can one person be contacted?
What do you want to compare between campaigns?
What happens to engagement data after the campaign?

name: Email Nurture
objective: Run a sequence that reacts to behaviour instead of a calendar.
commonProblems: The journey is a fixed schedule that ignores what the person did
Sales is not told when someone becomes interested
Exit conditions are missing, so a customer keeps receiving prospect content
Content is written once and never reviewed
Engagement is visible in the campaign tool but not on the CRM record
futureState: Exit on conversion, unsubscribe or completion
Engagement written back to the CRM
risks: Content does not exist and the client has not resourced it
Deliverability and domain authentication are outside the project's control
Sales ignores the handoff notification
discoveryQuestions: What is this journey trying to make happen?
Who leaves the journey, and why?
What does someone have to do before sales should call them?
Who is writing this content, and by when?
Is your sending domain authenticated?

name: WhatsApp & Messaging Journeys
objective: Use the channel the customer actually reads, within the rules that channel imposes.
commonProblems: Messaging happens on personal phones and leaves no record
Template approval is discovered as a dependency after the design is signed off
Marketing and support message the same customer from different numbers
Opt-in is assumed rather than captured
The 24-hour session window is not accounted for in the journey design
futureState: Journey triggered from a CRM event
Inbound replies routed to the right team as a conversation
Conversation logged against the customer record
controls: Approved templates only outside the session window
risks: Template and business-account approval sits with the channel provider and can delay go-live regardless of build readiness
Pricing per conversation changes the business case
Staff continue using personal numbers
discoveryQuestions: Are you approved for business messaging today, and under which number?
How is opt-in captured, and where is it stored?
Which messages are transactional and which are marketing?
Who answers when the customer replies?
What happens on the personal phones people use today?

name: Booking Journeys
objective: Let the customer book something real, against capacity that actually exists.
commonProblems: Availability is maintained manually and is wrong by lunchtime
Bookings arrive as messages and are entered by hand
Double-booking is prevented by memory
Cancellations and reschedules are untracked
No-shows have no consequence and no record
futureState: Availability published from a maintained source
Booking record created and linked to the customer
risks: Availability is maintained manually as a deliberate phase-1 decision, and drift is the accepted cost
Calendar integration assumptions differ per provider
Time zones and public holidays are underestimated
discoveryQuestions: Who maintains availability today, and how often is it wrong?
What is the real capacity constraint — people, rooms, equipment?
What is your cancellation policy, in writing?
What happens when someone does not turn up?
Does a booking need to become an invoice?

name: Campaign-to-Sales Handoff
objective: Close the loop between what marketing spent and what sales closed.
commonProblems: Marketing reports engagement; sales reports revenue; nobody joins them
Leads are handed over with no context about what the person responded to
Sales does not act on marketing-qualified leads and marketing never learns why
Attribution is argued about rather than measured
The definition of a qualified lead differs by department
futureState: Lead scored or flagged as marketing-qualified
Sales accepts or rejects with a reason
Rejected leads return to nurture with feedback
controls: Written definition of a qualified lead, agreed by both sides
reporting: Campaign contribution to pipeline and revenue
Time to first contact after handoff
risks: Attribution is a commercial argument that a system cannot settle
Sales rejects leads without a reason and the loop stays open
Scoring models are built on data the business does not reliably capture
discoveryQuestions: What does marketing consider a good lead, and what does sales consider one?
What happens the moment a lead is handed over?
How quickly must sales act, and what if they do not?
How do you want to attribute a closed deal to a campaign?
Who reviews this together, and how often?

name: Product Onboarding
objective: Get a product into the catalogue once, correctly, with the data every channel will demand.
commonProblems: Each channel wants different attributes and the data is assembled per channel by hand
SKUs are created inconsistently and duplicates appear
Images and copy arrive late and block listing
Dimensions and weight are missing until the first delivery fails
Nobody owns the master product record
futureState: SKU assigned under a defined convention
Published to the channels that qualify
risks: Marketplace attribute requirements change and are not negotiable
Merchants supply poor data and enrichment quietly becomes an internal cost
Two systems both create SKUs
discoveryQuestions: Which system owns the master product record?
What is the SKU convention, and who enforces it?
What does each channel demand that the others do not?
What blocks a listing today, most often?
Who checks the data before it goes live — and are they real?

name: Catalogue Approval
objective: Keep control of what appears under your brand without becoming the bottleneck.
commonProblems: Everything is approved by one person, and they go on leave
Approval criteria are not written down, so decisions look arbitrary
Rejections have no reason, so merchants resubmit the same item
Approved items change after approval without re-review
Restricted or non-compliant categories are caught by luck
futureState: Automated checks against rules (category, attributes, restricted items, pricing bounds)
Approve, reject with reason, or request changes
controls: Named approver role with a deputy
reporting: Approval versus rejection rates by merchant
risks: Manual approval is deliberately retained for brand control and becomes the throughput ceiling
Merchants game the criteria if they are published in full
Change-triggered re-review is forgotten in phase 1
discoveryQuestions: What must never appear under your brand?
Who approves, and who approves when they are away?
Should approval be manual by design, or is that a resourcing accident?
What can be checked automatically before a human looks?
What happens when an approved product is edited?

name: Order-to-Fulfilment
objective: One order state model from payment to doorstep, whatever the channel.
commonProblems: Each channel has its own order states, so 'shipped' means four different things
Stock is allocated on picking rather than on order, so items oversell
Support cannot tell a customer where the order is
Split shipments break the state model
Third-party merchant orders follow an entirely separate path
futureState: Dispatched with tracking to the customer
controls: Single order state model across channels
Allocation on confirmation, not on pick
Delivery confirmation as a controlled event
Exception path for partial and split orders
risks: Marketplace channels enforce their own state names and SLAs
Merchant-fulfilled orders sit outside your warehouse's control but inside your brand promise
Delivery confirmation from couriers is not always reliable, and payout may depend on it
discoveryQuestions: Show me every state an order can be in today, per channel.
When is stock committed — at order, at payment or at pick?
What does a customer see, and when?
What happens when only half the order can ship?
Who fulfils a third-party merchant's order, and who answers for it?
What event proves delivery?

name: Inventory Movement
objective: Know what you have, where it is, and why the number changed.
commonProblems: Stock figures differ between the sales system and the warehouse
Adjustments are made without a reason code
Reservations are informal, so the same unit is sold twice
Goods received are entered days later
Damaged and quarantined stock still shows as available
futureState: Returns received into a disposition state
controls: Location and status states (available, reserved, damaged, quarantined)
risks: Real-time synchronisation between systems is often assumed but constrained by API limits — near-real-time may be the honest answer
Stock accuracy is a discipline problem that a system will expose but not solve
Multi-warehouse is scoped out and then arrives anyway
discoveryQuestions: Which system is the truth for stock quantity?
How often is the physical count wrong, and by how much?
Who can adjust stock, and does the system ask why?
Does sales need live stock, or stock as at this morning?
How many warehouses will there be in eighteen months?

name: Courier Assignment
objective: Pick the right carrier by rule, not by whoever is on the phone.
commonProblems: Carrier is chosen by habit
Oversized or hazardous items are booked with a carrier who will not take them
Rates are compared manually, or not at all
Tracking numbers are pasted into emails
Failed deliveries return with no process
futureState: Consignment ready with validated logistics data
Carrier selected by rule (service level, geography, dimensions, cost, special handling)
controls: Mandatory dimensions, weight and address validation before booking
reporting: On-time delivery by carrier and route
risks: Address quality is the most common cause of failure and is a data problem upstream
Carrier APIs vary widely in quality and coverage
Rate cards are commercially sensitive and change
discoveryQuestions: How is a carrier chosen today, honestly?
What can each carrier not take?
Is address data validated anywhere before dispatch?
What happens when a delivery fails?
Who sees the cost of shipping, and when?

name: Delivery Management
objective: Make delivery a confirmed event, because other things depend on it.
commonProblems: Delivery is assumed from a tracking status rather than confirmed
Proof of delivery lives with the carrier and is requested by email
Payout, invoicing or warranty start from the wrong date
Failed and partial deliveries have no state
The customer is told nothing between dispatch and doorstep
futureState: In-transit visibility to customer and support
Delivery confirmed as a system event
Downstream triggers fired (payout, invoice, warranty start, feedback)
Exceptions handled (failed, refused, partial, damaged on arrival)
controls: Delivery confirmation as a controlled event, not an inference
Downstream triggers tied to confirmation only
risks: Carrier data quality determines whether confirmation is trustworthy
If payout depends on delivery, a wrong confirmation is a financial error
Proof-of-delivery retention may have contractual or regulatory implications
discoveryQuestions: What event proves a delivery happened?
What depends on that event — money, warranty, service start?
Where does proof of delivery live today?
What is a partial delivery in your system?
Who tells the customer, and when?

name: Return-to-Refund
objective: Connect the physical return to the financial one, in the right order.
commonProblems: Refunds are issued before goods are inspected, or weeks after
The refund does not reconcile to the original payment method
Instalment or third-party financed purchases have no defined refund path
Merchant-funded refunds are recovered informally, or not at all
Partial refunds are calculated by hand
futureState: Refund executed to the original payment method or provider
controls: Reconciliation to the financial source of truth
reporting: Refund volume and value by reason
risks: A financed purchase involves a third party whose refund process you do not control
The financial system, not the CRM, must remain the source of truth for money
Partial refunds interact badly with promotions and tax
discoveryQuestions: Who paid, and through what — you, a provider, or an instalment plan?
When is the customer entitled to their money back?
Which system creates the credit note?
Who absorbs the loss when it is a merchant's product?
What happens to an instalment agreement when the goods come back?

name: Merchant Onboarding
objective: Let third parties sell under your brand without inheriting their problems.
commonProblems: Merchants are onboarded by email and a spreadsheet
Verification is inconsistent, so risk is unknown
Commercial terms differ per merchant and are not recorded in any system
Merchants list before their logistics capability is verified
There is no way to suspend a merchant cleanly
futureState: Commercial terms agreed (commission, payout, service standards)
Ongoing performance review with a suspension path
controls: Terms recorded against the merchant record
Manual approval retained where brand risk is high
risks: Onboarding is deliberately manual for brand control, which caps growth — that is a commercial decision, not a technical one
Banking and verification data are sensitive and have handling requirements
Merchant performance standards are unenforceable without a suspension mechanism designed in from the start
discoveryQuestions: What makes a merchant acceptable to you?
What do you need to verify, and who checks it?
Do all merchants get the same commercial terms?
What are the service standards, and what happens when a merchant misses them?
How do you remove a merchant who is trading badly?

name: Merchant Payout & Commission
objective: Pay third parties the right amount, on a trigger everyone agreed to in advance.
commonProblems: The payout trigger is not defined — order, dispatch, delivery or return window?
Commission is calculated in a spreadsheet
Returns after payout create a recovery problem nobody designed for
Merchants dispute figures and there is no statement to point at
Financed sales pay out on a different rhythm and it was not modelled
futureState: Payout statement generated for the merchant
Ledger reconciled in the financial system of truth
controls: Payout trigger defined contractually and in the system
Commission rules held as data, not code
Reconciliation to the financial source of truth
dataRequired: Commission rate and rules per merchant
reporting: Payout value by period and merchant
risks: Money processes must reconcile to the financial system, not to the CRM
A wrong payout trigger creates a real financial exposure on returns
Commission structures change per merchant and per campaign, and hard-coding them guarantees rework
discoveryQuestions: What event earns the merchant their money?
How long is the return window, and do you hold payout for it?
How is commission structured — flat, tiered, per category?
Which system produces the ledger entry?
What happens when a paid-out order is returned?
How does a merchant dispute a figure?

name: Quote-to-Cash
objective: Follow one thread from quotation to money received, across however many systems own the steps.
commonProblems: The quote is in one system, the invoice in another, the payment in a third, and the thread is a person
Nobody can say how long it takes from deal to cash
Sales does not know the customer has not paid
The CRM has no financial visibility, so account management is blind
Reconciliation is a monthly manual exercise
futureState: Quotation produced in the pricing system of record
Quote accepted and recorded against the deal
Invoice raised in the accounting system of record
Payment recorded in the accounting system
State reflected against the customer and deal
controls: One source of truth per record type (quote, invoice, payment)
One-way state reflection into the CRM
No duplicate financial calculation in the CRM
dataRequired: Invoice number, value and due date
risks: Reflecting financial state into the CRM invites the assumption that the CRM can be edited — it cannot
Sync frequency between systems is a business decision (real-time versus periodic) with a real cost
Multi-currency and tax rules belong to the accounting system and should not be recreated
discoveryQuestions: Which system creates the quote, which creates the invoice, and which records the payment?
Does sales need to see payment status, and to do what with it?
How often does data need to move between systems — really?
What links a quote to an invoice today?
How long does deal-to-cash take now, and who could tell me?

name: Invoice & Payment Tracking
objective: Let the people who talk to the customer see whether the customer has paid.
commonProblems: Payment status lives only in the accounting system, which sales cannot open
Chasing is done from an exported spreadsheet
Part-paid invoices show as unpaid
Credit notes and adjustments are invisible outside finance
Customers are chased by two people on the same day
futureState: Invoice raised in the accounting system of record
Invoice reference and status reflected against the customer record
Payment recorded in the accounting system
controls: Accounting system remains the source of truth
Dispute state that stops automated chasing
dataRequired: Invoice number, value, currency and due date
risks: Reflecting financial data into a CRM raises access-control questions — not everyone should see everything
Sync lag creates awkward conversations if a customer paid this morning
Automated chasing on a disputed invoice damages the relationship
discoveryQuestions: Who currently knows whether a customer has paid?
Who should?
Which system is allowed to change payment status?
What is your chase sequence today, and who runs it?
What happens when the customer disputes an invoice?

name: Partial Payments & Deposits
objective: Model a payment schedule that reflects what the business actually agreed.
commonProblems: A deposit is recorded as a paid invoice
Work starts before the deposit clears, sometimes
The balance is chased manually at an unpredictable moment
Deposit calculation rules differ per job type and live in someone's head
Refunding a deposit has no defined path
futureState: Terms agreed with a payment schedule
Milestone or balance invoices raised on defined triggers
controls: Deposit received as a hard gate before work starts
Payment schedule held against the agreement
Calculation rules as configuration, not code
risks: Deposit calculation rules are often more complex than first described and drive real scope
The 'work cannot start' gate is a business rule people will want to override
Cancellation and refund terms are frequently undefined until the first cancellation
discoveryQuestions: What does a customer pay, and when?
How is the deposit calculated — and are there exceptions?
Can work start before the deposit clears? Who decides?
What triggers the balance invoice?
What happens if they cancel after paying a deposit?

name: Instalment Checkout
objective: Offer instalments without becoming a lender.
commonProblems: The business drifts toward building a credit engine it is not licensed to operate
Eligibility is displayed before the provider has decided
Who chases a missed repayment is undefined
The order is confirmed before the finance decision returns
Servicing and collections conversations arrive at the same support desk with no distinction
futureState: Application passed to the external finance provider
Decision returned and reflected on the order
controls: No credit logic inside the commerce system
Order confirmation gated on the provider's decision
Written responsibility split for repayment, servicing and collections
Decline handled without exposing the reason
reporting: Support contacts by type (servicing versus repayment)
risks: Regulatory exposure if the retailer appears to make lending decisions
Provider downtime blocks checkout — a fallback must be a decision, not an omission
Customers will call the retailer about repayments regardless of the contract, so the routing must exist
Refunds on a financed order must be agreed with the provider before launch
discoveryQuestions: Who makes the lending decision, and are they licensed to?
What does the customer see while the decision is pending?
Who does the customer call about a missed payment — you or the provider?
What happens at checkout if the provider is down?
How does a refund work on a financed purchase?
Is any of this regulated in your market?

name: Commission Management
objective: Calculate what people and partners are owed from data, not from a spreadsheet.
commonProblems: Commission is calculated monthly by hand and disputed monthly by everyone
Rules change per campaign and are not versioned
Clawback on returns or cancellations is informal
Split credit between two salespeople has no model
The rule is embedded in code and every change is a development request
futureState: Qualifying event occurs (won, delivered, paid — defined explicitly)
Commission calculated against the applicable rule version
Payment executed via payroll or accounts
controls: Commission rules held as configurable data with version history
Clawback rules defined at design time
reporting: Commission by period, person and merchant
risks: Rules encoded as code guarantee a change request every quarter
The qualifying event is often contested — won, delivered or paid are very different cash positions
Historical recalculation is expensive if rule versions are not retained
discoveryQuestions: What event earns commission — the sale, the delivery or the payment?
How often do the rules change?
What happens when the order is returned after commission is paid?
How is credit split when two people worked the deal?
Who approves a statement before anyone is paid?

name: Subscription & Renewal Billing
objective: Bill recurring revenue on a schedule the system owns.
commonProblems: Recurring invoices are raised manually and get missed
Mid-term upgrades are pro-rated by hand
Failed payments have no retry or dunning path
Cancellation mid-cycle has no defined refund position
Renewal price increases are applied inconsistently
futureState: Subscription created with terms and schedule
Revenue recognised in the accounting system
controls: Billing schedule owned by one system
risks: Involuntary churn from card failures is usually larger than expected
Pro-ration is a rules problem that grows with every plan variant
Revenue recognition is an accounting decision, not a system default
discoveryQuestions: What plans exist, and what really differs between them?
What happens when a payment fails — how many times, over how long?
Can a customer change plan mid-cycle, and who works out the money?
What are the cancellation terms?
Who is allowed to change a price?

name: Multi-Company & Multi-Currency Operations
objective: Run several legal entities and currencies without four disconnected businesses.
commonProblems: Each entity runs its own system and consolidation is a spreadsheet
The same customer exists three times with three different balances
Exchange rates are entered manually and inconsistently
Inter-company transactions are recorded on one side only
Reporting cannot separate entity performance from group performance
futureState: Customer and product data governed at the level that makes sense (shared or per entity)
Transactions raised in the entity's own base currency
Inter-company transactions recorded on both sides
risks: Fiscal years, tax rules and holiday calendars differ per country and are often assumed identical
Permission separation between entities is a compliance issue, not a preference
Consolidation logic belongs in the accounting or reporting layer, not the CRM
discoveryQuestions: How many legal entities are there, and will that number change?
Do the entities share customers, products or people?
What is each entity's base currency and fiscal year?
Where do exchange rates come from?
Who is allowed to see another entity's data?

name: Landed Cost
objective: Know what the product really cost by the time it reached your shelf.
commonProblems: Margin is calculated on supplier price alone
Freight, duty and clearing are booked as overheads and never reach the SKU
Costs arrive weeks after the goods and nobody restates the cost
Currency movement between order and payment is ignored
Pricing decisions are made on a number that is not true
futureState: Goods shipped with associated costs identified
Freight, duty, clearing and handling captured
Costs allocated to shipment lines by an agreed basis (value, weight, volume)
controls: All cost components captured before valuation
reporting: True margin by SKU and shipment
Variance between estimated and actual landed cost
risks: Costs genuinely arrive late, so the process needs a restatement path rather than a pretence of accuracy
Allocation basis is an accounting decision that changes the answer
This belongs in the inventory or accounting system, not in a CRM
discoveryQuestions: What does the product cost by the time it can be sold?
Which costs do you currently ignore?
How do you allocate freight across mixed shipments?
When do you find out the real cost, and does anyone go back and fix it?
What margin figure are you pricing against today?

name: Project Delivery
objective: Run the work in phases with owners, dependencies and a definition of done.
commonProblems: Scope is agreed verbally and remembered differently
Phases exist on the plan but everything runs at once
Dependencies on the client are not tracked as risks
Change requests are absorbed as goodwill until the margin disappears
Status reporting is optimistic until it is not
futureState: Phases with entry and exit criteria
controls: Written definition of done per phase
Dependency register with owners and dates
risks: Client dependencies are the most common cause of slippage and the least tracked
Change absorbed as a defect destroys the estimate silently
Phase boundaries erode under commercial pressure
discoveryQuestions: What is the approved scope, and where is it written?
What does the client owe this project, and by when?
What is 'done' for this phase?
Who decides whether something is a defect or a change?
What happens if a dependency is two weeks late?

name: Approval Management
objective: Put approvals where risk is, and nowhere else.
commonProblems: Everything needs approval, so approval means nothing
The approver is a person, not a role, and they go on leave
Approvals happen in email and are not attached to the record
Thresholds are undocumented
There is no record of who approved what, or when
futureState: Request raised with the data needed to decide
Approval requirement determined by rule (value, type, risk)
Routed to a role with a deputy
controls: Approval matrix by value and type
risks: Approval layers are often organisational habit rather than control, and removing them is political
Routing to individuals rather than roles breaks the first time someone is on leave
Approvals designed for management comfort become the bottleneck
discoveryQuestions: What is each approval actually protecting against?
What would happen if this one disappeared?
Who approves when the approver is away?
What is the value threshold, and who set it?
Does anyone ever need to prove who approved something?

name: Field Operations & Job Lifecycle
objective: Get the right person, with the right parts, to the right place — and capture what happened.
commonProblems: Scheduling is a whiteboard and a phone
Technicians are dispatched without the skills or parts the job needs
Job completion is a photograph in a WhatsApp group
Different job types follow the same workflow even though they are not the same work
Invoicing waits for paperwork to come back from the van
futureState: Job created from an order, contract or ticket
Work performed and recorded on site
controls: Job type drives the workflow, not one generic path
Skill and part checks before dispatch
Time and parts captured at source
risks: Different job types genuinely need different workflows — forcing one blueprint is a common and expensive shortcut
Address validation and skill-based scheduling are frequently underestimated at scoping
Mobile capture depends on connectivity that may not exist on site
discoveryQuestions: What are the genuinely different job types, and how does the work differ?
What has to be true before a technician is dispatched?
What is captured on site today, and on what?
What does the customer sign, if anything?
What triggers the invoice?
Does the technician have signal where the work happens?

name: Mileage & Route Tracking
objective: Record travel accurately enough to reimburse, plan and justify it.
commonProblems: Mileage is claimed from memory at month end
There is no route planning, so the day is inefficient by default
Reimbursement rules differ per person
Claims are approved without evidence
Travel time is invisible in capacity planning
futureState: Start and end recorded against a job or purpose
Distance calculated from a defined source
Claim submitted with the job reference
controls: Rate policy per vehicle or staff category
reporting: Mileage by person, vehicle and job type
risks: Location tracking has privacy and employee-relations implications that are not a technical decision
Distance sources disagree, and the difference is money
Manual capture will be gamed if it is easier than being accurate
discoveryQuestions: What are you trying to achieve — reimbursement, planning or cost visibility?
How is mileage claimed today?
What is the policy rate, and does it vary?
Are staff comfortable with location capture, and have they been asked?
Should travel time count against job capacity?

name: Asset Management
objective: Know what you own, where it is, who has it and what has been done to it.
commonProblems: Assets live in a spreadsheet that is out of date
Custody is not recorded, so nobody knows who has it
Maintenance is reactive because schedules are not tracked
Asset history is spread across tickets, jobs and email
Disposal is not recorded and the register never shrinks
futureState: Asset registered with a unique identity
Assigned to a location, person or customer
Maintenance scheduled and performed against the asset
Incidents and service history attached to the asset
controls: Custody transfer as a recorded event
History attached to the asset, not the ticket
reporting: Assets by category, status and location
risks: The initial register is usually built from an unreliable spreadsheet and needs a data project of its own
Custody discipline is behavioural; the system only records it
Finance and operations often want different asset definitions
discoveryQuestions: What counts as an asset here — and at what value?
Where does the register live today, and when was it last true?
Who is allowed to move an asset?
What maintenance is scheduled, and who tracks it?
What happens when an asset is scrapped?

name: Vehicle Management
objective: Keep the fleet legal, available and accounted for.
commonProblems: Licence, insurance and inspection dates are tracked in a calendar reminder
Vehicle allocation is informal
Damage is discovered by the next driver
Service history is with the garage
Fuel and running costs are not attributed to the vehicle
futureState: Allocated to a driver or purpose
Service and maintenance scheduled and recorded
controls: Compliance date monitoring with lead time
reporting: Incident and damage rate by driver and vehicle
Cost per vehicle and per kilometre
risks: Compliance requirements vary by jurisdiction and are legal facts, not preferences
Driver behaviour capture has employee-relations implications
Existing records are usually incomplete and need cleansing
discoveryQuestions: What must be current for this vehicle to be legal to drive?
Who allocates vehicles, and how?
How do you find out about damage?
Where is the service history?
Do you know what each vehicle costs you?

name: Vendor Management
objective: Treat suppliers as a managed relationship rather than a purchase order.
commonProblems: Vendors are onboarded by whoever needs them, with no verification
Terms are in a contract nobody can find
Performance is discussed but never measured
The same vendor exists three times under different names
Compliance documents expire unnoticed
controls: Terms recorded against the vendor record
risks: Vendor master data is usually duplicated across finance and operations
Performance criteria are easy to define and hard to collect
Compliance requirements differ by category and jurisdiction
discoveryQuestions: Who can bring in a new vendor?
What do you verify before you buy from someone?
Where are the agreed terms?
How would you know if a vendor is performing badly?
What documents expire, and who watches them?

name: Quality Control
objective: Catch the problem at the step where it is cheapest to fix.
commonProblems: Quality is checked at the end, when rework is most expensive
Inspection results are recorded on paper forms and filed
Trial and first-article cycles are informal
External departments and suppliers participate but have no access
Root cause is not linked to the corrective action
futureState: Quality criteria defined per item or stage
controls: Non-conformance cannot be closed without root cause and verification
reporting: Non-conformance by type, stage and supplier
risks: External departments and suppliers need controlled participation without full system access
Paper forms persist because they work on the shop floor — the replacement must be at least as fast
Trial cycles are iterative by nature and resist linear workflow design
discoveryQuestions: Where in the process do you find problems today, and where should you?
What is recorded on paper, and why has that survived?
Who outside your company takes part in this process?
What happens to material that fails?
Is a corrective action ever verified, or just closed?

name: Contract Management
objective: Know what you have agreed, with whom, and what happens next.
commonProblems: Contracts are PDFs in a folder structure only one person understands
Key dates and obligations are not extracted anywhere
The billing party and the operating party are assumed to be the same
Amendments are separate files with no link to the original
Nobody knows which terms apply to a given transaction
futureState: Contract requested and drafted from an approved template
Terms negotiated and recorded as data, not only prose
Signed and stored with the record
controls: Key terms captured as structured data
risks: The counterparty structure is often more complex than 'a customer' — several parties can have different roles on one agreement
Extracting terms as data from legacy contracts is a manual project
Access control on contracts is a genuine confidentiality requirement
discoveryQuestions: Who are the parties, and does each one do the same thing?
Who pays, who signs, who operates — the same entity, or not?
What dates matter, and who watches them?
Where do amendments live?
Who is allowed to read this contract?

name: Licence & Certification Management
objective: Issue, track and renew the permissions the business depends on.
commonProblems: The licensee, the owner, the operator and the payer are different parties and the system models one 'customer'
Renewal depends on a person remembering
Transfer of a licence between parties has no process
Supporting documents are collected by email
The register cannot answer 'who is responsible for this asset today?'
futureState: Party roles identified and verified (owner, operator, licensee, billing party, broker)
Licence issued against the asset with a validity period
Transfer, suspension or revocation handled as defined events
controls: Party-role model rather than a single customer field
risks: Relationship modelling is the whole problem: several companies can hold different roles against one asset simultaneously, and each role can change independently
Standard CRM objects rarely express this cleanly and the temptation is to flatten it
History matters — who held which role, when — and is easy to lose
discoveryQuestions: Who can be involved with one asset, and in what capacity?
Can one company hold more than one role at once?
Who receives the invoice, and is that ever a different party?
What happens when the asset is sold?
Do you need to know who was responsible last year?

name: Role & Permission Governance
objective: Decide who can see and do what — before the system decides for you.
commonProblems: Everyone is an administrator because it was faster
Permissions are granted per person and never reviewed
Data visibility follows the org chart on paper, not in the system
Leavers keep access
Sensitive data (financial, personal, commercial) is visible by default
futureState: Roles defined from the process, not the org chart
Profiles defining what each role can do
Data-sharing rules defining what each role can see
Access granted by role on joining
risks: Permission design is done last and then retrofitted, which is when the exceptions appear
Multi-entity businesses need separation that the default model does not provide
Restricting managers' visibility is politically difficult and must be decided by the client
discoveryQuestions: Who should not be able to see this?
Is that a rule, or is it just how it happens to be today?
What happens to access when someone leaves?
Who has administrator rights right now, and why?
Does any of this data have a legal restriction on it?

name: UAT & Change Control
objective: Make acceptance a decision that was defined before the build started.
commonProblems: UAT is run by the people who wrote the requirement
There is no written definition of a defect versus a change request
Testing is a demo, so only the happy path is exercised
Issues are raised in a chat thread and lost
Sign-off is verbal and later disputed
futureState: Acceptance criteria defined at requirement time
Test scenarios written from requirements, not from the build
Role-based execution by real business users
Issues logged and classified as defect or change
Changes assessed for impact and decided
controls: Defect versus change definition agreed in writing
Impact assessment before any change is accepted
risks: Absorbing changes as defects destroys the estimate quietly and is the most common commercial failure in delivery
Business users are not released from their day job for UAT
Test data that is not realistic hides the real defects
discoveryQuestions: Who will actually test this — by name?
What is a defect here, and what is a change? Where is that written?
What data will they test with?
What has to pass before go-live is allowed?
Who signs, and what are they signing?
